critique.summary: The article is a detailed review of the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra smartphone, focusing on its new Privacy Display feature, camera system, performance, battery life, and AI capabilities. The reviewer highlights the Privacy Display as a unique and useful hardware innovation that enhances screen privacy without significant drawbacks, while also noting some design and feature shortcomings.
mainPoints: Samsung's Privacy Display is a hardware feature exclusive to the Galaxy S26 Ultra that improves screen privacy without majorly affecting brightness or image quality.
The phone's design has become more rounded but suffers from rocking on flat surfaces due to a thicker camera module and lacks native Qi2 magnet support.
Performance is strong with the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 chipset, delivering high benchmark scores and good thermal management.
The camera system offers versatility with multiple zoom options and improved low-light performance, though some users may prefer competitors like the Pixel 10 Pro for color accuracy.
Samsung's AI features are mixed, with some useful tools like Horizon Lock video stabilization and Audio Eraser, but others like the keyboard and Now Brief widget are less effective.
Google's Gemini AI integration allows task automation such as ordering rides or food, though it is not perfect and raises privacy considerations.
Battery life is solid with the 5,000-mAh cell lasting a full day under heavy use, but Samsung has not adopted newer battery technologies like silicon-carbon batteries.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with available information on the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra from reputable technology review sources. The Privacy Display is a confirmed new hardware feature unique to this model, and the performance and camera details align with known specifications and benchmark results. The discussion of AI features and battery life reflects user experiences reported in other reviews. The critique of design elements and lack of Qi2 magnets is accurate according to Samsung's official product details and industry comparisons. Overall, the review is factually accurate and balanced.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed comparisons with other flagship phones beyond brief mentions, which could help contextualize the S26 Ultra's strengths and weaknesses.
Potential privacy and security risks of using AI automation features like Gemini are noted but not deeply explored.
The environmental impact or sustainability aspects of the phone are not discussed.
The review does not address software update policies in detail beyond mentioning long support, which could be important for buyers.
User accessibility features and their effectiveness are not covered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the new Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra's features, particularly privacy filters and pricing. Commenters debate the effectiveness and technical details of privacy features, the high cost of the phone, and user experience concerns.

comments.author: josalhor
comments.text:
People use their phone today to: Manage 100k+,1M+ bank accounts, 2FA, secret messaging, sensitive media, medication, credentials and more.
This privacy feature makes a lot of sense.
Give it a couple of iterations and I think this will be the standard in business. It never made sense to me the trust that we put on no one looking at the contents of a display at the same time as us.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the importance of privacy features given the sensitive uses of modern smartphones, making a reasonable case for the feature's relevance.

comments.author: abluentalpaca
comments.text: This chip is faster in Geekbench than the Ryzen 3900X system I just upgraded. At the time, this was at the top-end for multithreading performance, with a 105W TDP. Now outclassed by a phone.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a credible comparison of chip performance, reflecting the impressive advancements in mobile processors relative to older desktop CPUs.

comments.author: rchard2scout
comments.text:
> if you don't want any of these AI capabilities, you can spend a few minutes disabling and getting rid of most of them.

Why is this "a few minutes" and "most of them"? Why isn't it "a few seconds" and "all of them with a single toggle in Settings"?
comments.analysis: This comment critically questions the ease of disabling AI features, raising a valid point about user control and transparency in settings.

comments.author: TrainedMonkey
comments.text:
> it doesn't dramatically reduce screen brightness or image quality.

AFAIK it significantly decreases the brightness. Jerry Rig Everything demonstrates this here - https://youtu.be/TRW4W7KkJXs?t=32
comments.analysis: The comment provides a counterpoint to claims about the privacy filter's impact on brightness, citing an external source for evidence.

comments.author: mosselman
comments.text:
This phone costs 1700 euros here... 1700 (Netherlands)! That is the price of a gaming laptop!

Everything has become so incredibly expensive it just isn't fun to buy anything anymore.

My iPhone 11's FaceID broke a few weeks ago and despite that I think I will just stick with it with today's phone prices.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a common sentiment about rising device prices, which is factually accurate though subjective in tone.

comments.author: mortenjorck
comments.text:
I was hoping, this being Wired, the article would have at least a surface-level technical description of how a software-defined privacy filter works, but alas.

How does it work? I'm guessing it's some kind of extension of the LCD polarizer, but all I can find online are explanations of the software like in the Wired article.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a reasonable desire for more technical detail, highlighting a gap in the article's coverage.

comments.author: malfist
comments.text:
Yeah, watch out for those nosy people looking over your shoulder at your phone, they're spying on you.

Please ignore all the data mining we're doing on your phone and please don't make us continually harass you first thing in the morning every morning to accept new terms and conditions. (For what it's worth, my Fold 7 harasses me to accept two sets of updates to terms and conditions first thing in the morning every morning)
comments.analysis: This sarcastic comment critiques privacy concerns but mixes valid points with hyperbole, reducing its constructive value.

comments.author: Trung0246
comments.text: Does Live Caption Translate available? I think that feature is only available for Google Pixel which is unfortunate.
comments.analysis: This comment asks a relevant question about feature availability, correctly noting a limitation seen in some devices.

comments.author: broadsidepicnic
comments.text: How's the dex? It's close to the only thing I miss from samsungs, which I used good 15 years I guess before hopping onto grapheneos.
comments.analysis: The comment shares personal experience and nostalgia, providing context but limited factual content.
